---
title: "Ongoing Housing stress and cognitive functions - manuscript"
author: "Takashi Iida"
format:
  pdf:
    fontsize: 12pt
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1in
      - right=1in
  docx: default
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
---

## Introduction

Housing is a recognized driver of health disparities [@Swope2019-gm]. In the World Health Organization's 2010 framework, housing was classified as an intermediary determinant of health—downstream from structural forces yet directly shaping health outcomes [@Solar2010-ix]. Its three key elements; affordability, suitability, and security [@Mansour2022-ai], when unmet, expose residents to homelessness, housing instability, and foreclosure. Housing insecurity in the United States is widespread and growing: more than one third of community-living older Americans report at least one form of it, including poor affordability, poor quality, or poor neighborhood conditions [@Paredes2024-zz]. In 2024, more than 700,000 people experienced homelessness—the highest count since 2007 [@De_Sousa2024-br]. Older adults face particular vulnerability, and the population aged 65 and older is projected to grow rapidly in coming decades [@Culhane_undated-tn]. Housing problems also harm communities: rising foreclosure rates erode local property tax revenues [@Apgar2005-td], and neighborhood eviction is linked to higher crime rates [@Schwartz2024-po].

Housing problems generate chronic psychological stress [@Talmatzky2026-dy; @Singh2019-qo; @Dotsikas2023-ex], which contributes to several adverse health outcomes. Housing disadvantage is associated with poor mental health [@Singh2019-qo], depression [@Kingsbury2018-yp], and—for those who perceive a risk of eviction—anxiety and related medication use [@Acharya2022-ux]. Housing instability also predicts adverse cardiometabolic outcomes [@Gu2023-xz]. 

Emerging evidence further suggests that housing stress may accelerate memory decline: foreclosure, for example, has been associated with faster cognitive decline among older adults [@Kim2024-se; @Friedman2021-jz]. Biologically, chronic stress may impair memory through dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and prolonged cortisol exposure. [@Lupien1998-jj]This process has been linked to hippocampal atrophy and poorer hippocampus-dependent memory. [@Sapolsky2000-hm] Cognitive decline in midlife and late life increases the risk of dementia and Alzheimer's disease [@Dhana2025-pq; @Belleville2017-sr], making it important to identify modifiable contributing factors. Cognitive decline varies by domain; two domains especially relevant to Alzheimer's disease are episodic memory and working memory. Episodic memory, assessed through immediate and delayed word recall, is an early cognitive marker with high diagnostic accuracy for Alzheimer's disease [@Weissberger2017-vb]. Working memory, which reflects attentional control and central executive function, is also commonly impaired in Alzheimer's disease [@Kirova2015-ix; @Stopford2012-bs]. Studying these domains clarifies whether housing-related stressors contribute to dementia-relevant cognitive change.

The cognitive effects of housing stress may also differ by sex. Women score higher on immediate and delayed word recall in older age [@Golchert2019-un] yet decline faster in global cognition and executive function than men [@Levine2021-pj]. On the other hand, perceived stress may cause faster cognitive decline for men than women. [@Paolillo2023-he], suggesting that sex may modify the cognitive effects of stress.

Prior research links housing conditions to cognitive health. Better housing quality predicts higher executive function [@Vaid2024-my], and housing distress is associated with increased cognitive impairment risk [@Wilkinson2026-wu]. However, whether housing stress causally contributes to cognitive decline remains unclear [@Walsh2025-hj]. Few studies assess episodic and working memory separately, and sex differences in these effects remain poorly characterized. Using a longitudinal trajectory approach, this study examines the domain-specific effects of ongoing housing stress on episodic memory and working memory separately among U.S. adults aged 50 and older.  We also examine whether sex modifies this association, given evidence that women and men differ in both cognitive trajectories and stress sensitivity. We hypothesize that greater housing stress accelerates cognitive decline in both domains, with stronger effects among women than men.


## Method

### Data Collection

The Health and Retirement Study (HRS) is a nationally representative cohort of U.S. adults aged 50 and older, following approximately 20,000 participants across the United States over time. [@Sonnega2014-my] The initial HRS cohort included individuals born between 1931 and 1941, who were first interviewed in 1992 (wave 1). [@Bugliari2025-jb] HRS has conducted core interviews with trained interviewers every two years since 1992. [@Fisher2018-cl] Before 2004, HRS conducted follow-up interviews primarily by telephone, with face-to-face interviews for respondents over the age of 80. [@Sonnega2014-my] Beginning in 2006, HRS assigned half of the cohort to an enhanced face-to-face interview that included physical and biological measures and a psychosocial questionnaire, such that all participants received enhanced face-to-face interviews every four years. [@Fisher2018-cl] The HRS uses a multi-stage national probability sampling and adds a new cohort of individuals aged 51–56 every six years (e.g., in 1998, 2004, 2010, 2016). [@Fisher2018-cl; @Juster1995-oa; @Willis1999-nb]

### Analytic Sample

We used wave 8 (2006) as the baseline because the HRS began collecting ongoing housing stress data in that year. [@Smith2023-xv] Of the 45,234 participants across all waves, 18,469 were wave 8 respondents after excluding non-wave-8 respondents (n=26,765). We further excluded respondents with a doctor-diagnosed memory-related disease at baseline (n=978), leaving 17,491 eligible participants. We then excluded those lacking ongoing housing stress data at wave 8 (n=10,304), yielding 7,187 participants. An additional 90 participants were excluded due to missing cognitive data at wave 8, resulting in a final analytic sample of 7,097. We included only respondents and excluded spouses/partners to avoid within-household clustering, since couples share the same housing environment and may have correlated exposures. Participants were followed for eight years across waves 8, 10, and 12. Wave 14 data were not included because non-missing exposure data at that wave represented only 2,800 observations, reflecting 62% missingness relative to the wave 8 sample. Figure 1 summarizes the flow of study participants.

### Exposure

In HRS data, there are items evaluating respondent's exposure to and perception of common chronic stressors. [@Smith2023-xv] As one of these stressors, we used ongoing housing stress as an exposure variable. Responses indicate whether a housing problem has lasted at least twelve months and how upsetting it has been, using four categories: “1 = No, didn't happen,” “2 = Yes, but not upsetting,” “3 = Yes, somewhat upsetting,” and “4 = Yes, very upsetting.” We dichotomized responses into “yes” (categories 2–4) versus “no” (category 1). We assessed the exposure at each wave (waves 8, 10, and 12) through self-report. We handled ongoing housing stress as a single item, consistent with the HRS Psychosocial and Lifestyle Questionnaire user guide. [@Smith2023-xv]

### Outcomes

As outcome variables, we used two domains in cognitive functions: episodic memory and working memory. We assessed episodic memory using total word recall, which was the sum of immediate and delayed word recall scores. For immediate word recall, the interviewer read a randomly assigned list of 10 nouns from four non-overlapping lists and asked the respondent to recall as many words as possible in any order. The scores ranged from 0 to 10, which equaled the number of words correctly recalled. [@McCammon2023-oa] Across waves, each respondent received a different list, and household partners were assigned lists so they would not receive the same list in the same or adjacent waves. Delayed word recall was assessed approximately five minutes after the immediate recall task, when respondents were asked to recall the previously presented nouns. The scores also ranged from 0 to 10. [@McCammon2023-oa] We assessed working memory using the serial 7's score. In serial 7's task, the interviewer asked the respondent to subtract 7 from 100 five times consecutively; scores ranged from 0 to 5. [@McCammon2023-oa] Each subtraction was scored independently. For example, if a respondent answered the first item incorrectly (e.g. 92) but correctly subtracted 7 from their own answer for the remaining four trials (e.g. 85, 78, 71, 64), the person would receive a score of 4. The interviewer did not repeat the respondent's responses during the task. Because total word recall and serial 7's are on different scales, we standardized both into z-scores, consistent with prior longitudinal cognitive research. [@Andrade2021-gj; @Song2025-zl; @Hahn2015-yw]

### Confounders

We selected and operationalized covariates based on HRS documentation. [@Bugliari2025-jb] We calculated age in years by dividing the number of months between the respondent's birth date and interview date by 12. We treated sex (male or female) as an effect measure modifier. We combined race and ethnicity into five categories: Non-Hispanic White, Non-Hispanic Black, Hispanic, Non-Hispanic Other, and Other. We grouped education into five categories: less than high school diploma, General Educational Development (GED), high school diploma, some college (less than a bachelor's degree), and college and above. Total household income was used in nominal dollars. We coded current smoking status as yes or no. We constructed an eight-condition comorbidity index in which we coded each condition as 1 if the respondent ever reported a physician diagnosis in the current or any prior wave, and 0 otherwise. The eight conditions were hypertension, diabetes or high blood sugar, cancer or malignant tumor (excluding skin cancer), chronic lung disease (excluding asthma), heart disease (including heart attack, coronary heart disease, angina, congestive heart failure, or other heart problems), stroke or transient ischemic attack, emotional, nervous, or psychiatric problems, and arthritis or rheumatism. [@Pan2023-cy] The summed index ranged from 0 to 8, with higher scores indicating greater disease burden. We used baseline values for sex, race and ethnicity, and education. We treated age, total household income, smoking status, and comorbidity index as time-varying covariates measured at each wave.

### Statistical Analysis

We summarized baseline characteristics of the analytic sample using means (SD) or medians (IQR) for continuous variables and counts (percentages) for categorical variables. For the main analysis, we used individual fixed-effects models to estimate standardized mean differences (coefficients) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Model 1 was an unadjusted (crude) model, and Model 2 was adjusted for age, total household income, and comorbidity index. Sex and education were not included as covariates because fixed-effects models control for observed and unobserved time-invariant characteristics by exploiting within-person variation. [@Allison2005-vm; @Quintana2021-ng] For the subgroup analysis, we stratified data by sex to examine effect measure modification and applied crude and adjusted models separately to male and female subsamples. For missing cognitive scores, we used the imputed cognition data provided by HRS. [@McCammon2023-oa] Missing values, refusals, and not-applicable responses were imputed using a multivariate, regression-based procedure conducted with Imputation and Variance Estimation (IVEware) software, drawing on a set of relevant demographic, health, and economic variables along with cognitive scores from the prior and current waves. “Don't know” responses were treated as incorrect and were not imputed. Household income was used as reported in the RAND HRS dataset, where missing values were imputed by RAND using the unfolding brackets methodology. [@Bugliari2025-jb] We handled remaining missing data in the dataset using multiple imputation (m = 20) with results pooled via Rubin's rules. As a sensitivity analysis, we reran the primary models treating ongoing housing stress as an ordinal variable (1–4) to assess whether collapsing the "not upsetting" and "very upsetting" response categories affected the magnitude or direction of estimates. All analyses were conducted in R 4.5.1.




## Results

Table 1 shows characteristics of participants by housing stress status at baseline. The final analytic sample included 7,097 participants: 732 reported ongoing housing stress and 6,365 did not. At baseline, participants had a mean age of 67 years (SD 11) and were predominantly female (59%). Compared with those without ongoing housing stress, participants with ongoing housing stress were younger (mean age 65 vs 68 years) and more often female (62% vs 59%). In the ongoing housing stress group, less-than-high-school education was more common (28% vs 16%), college or above was less common (13% vs 23%), median household income was lower ($25,050 [IQR $12,526–$47,050] vs $42,540 [IQR $22,896–$80,520]), current smoking was more prevalent (23% vs 12%), and comorbidity burden was higher (mean 2.26 vs 1.86). Baseline cognitive performance was lower in the ongoing housing stress group across all three cognitive test scores: immediate word recall (mean 5.23 vs 5.57), delayed word recall (4.05 vs 4.46), and Serial 7's (3.08 vs 3.70).
